title: Epididymo-Orchitis
oneLiner: Acute infection/inflammation of epididymis +/- testis — young sexually active or older men with UTI risk; missed torsion = testicular loss.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Testicular torsion** – sudden onset (hours), severe pain, no fever, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testicle, negative Prehn's sign (pain unchanged with elevation). **Doppler ultrasound shows absent flow** → emergency exploration within 6 hours.
- **Fournier’s gangrene** – necrotising fasciitis of perineum; crepitus on palpation, systemic toxicity (tachycardia, hypotension, high lactate), dusky skin. **Immediate surgical debridement + IV antibiotics** – do not wait for imaging if clinical suspicion.
- **Sepsis** – fever, rigors, hypotension, raised lactate. **Start IV antibiotics and fluids within 1 hour** after blood cultures.
sections.Presentation:
- **Symptoms:**
  - Gradual (over hours–days) unilateral testicular pain, swelling, erythema
  - Dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency
  - Urethral discharge (if STI-related)
  - Fever, malaise
- **Signs** (examine standing):
  - Swollen, tender epididymis (palpable separate from testis early; later both are involved)
  - Reactive hydrocele (transilluminates)
  - Prehn's sign: pain relieved by elevating scrotum (supports epididymitis, not torsion) – *not reliable alone, use Doppler*
  - Urethral discharge (milking urethra)
  - Suprapubic tenderness (if concurrent prostatitis)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Testicular torsion** ↔ sudden onset, no urinary symptoms, absent Doppler flow, high-riding testis
- **Testicular tumour** ↔ painless, hard, irregular mass, no fever/urinary symptoms, Doppler shows vascularity
- **Incarcerated/strangulated inguinal hernia** ↔ above the scrotum, not reducible, bowel sounds, obstruction symptoms
- **Acute hydrocele** ↔ fluctuant, transilluminates, non-tender
- **Mumps orchitis** ↔ post-pubertal, parotitis, bilateral (uncommon)
- **Trauma** ↔ history, bruising, Doppler normal
- **Splitter:** **Prehn's sign + Doppler ultrasound** – pain relief with scrotal elevation + normal/ increased Doppler flow = epididymitis; no relief + absent flow = torsion.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urinalysis (dipstick) – nitrites, leukocytes, blood
  - Urine pregnancy test if relevant (not primary) – actually skip; not needed here
  - Check testicular lie and cremasteric reflex
  - NEWS2 chart, temperature, blood pressure
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (white cell count, neutrophils)
  - CRP
  - Urea & Creatinine (if suspected sepsis or obstruction)
  - Blood cultures if febrile/septic
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line: Scrotal ultrasound with colour Doppler** – assess blood flow, size, abscess, pyocele
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis if suspect abscess or complicated UTI (older men with obstruction)
- **Special tests:**
  - **STI screen** (≤35 years or sexually active): first-void urine for *Chlamydia trachomatis* + *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* NAAT; urethral swab if discharge; also HIV/ syphilis serology
  - **Mid-stream urine culture** (MSU) – before antibiotics
- **Never-miss test:** **Scrotal Doppler ultrasound** – omission risks missing torsion (medicolegal gold standard). If unavailable in hours, discuss with urology/radiology – do not wait >6 hours if torsion suspected clinically.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g IV/PO + ibuprofen 400mg PO (if no contraindication)
  - Scrotal support (elevated using towel/underwear)
  - **If torsion suspected:** inform urology immediately – do not wait for ultrasound if clinical suspicion high; transfer to theatre for exploration
  - **If signs of sepsis:** IV fluids (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline 500mL stat, repeat as per BP), IV antibiotics after cultures; escalate to sepsis 6
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Antibiotics according to presumed cause (see Drugs below)
  - If severe: continue IV until afebrile 48h, then switch to PO
  - Drainage of abscess (urology) if ultrasound shows collection
  - Monitor for urinary retention (common if prostatitis) – may need catheter
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete antibiotic course (usually 14 days)
  - Treat partner if STI diagnosed (chlamydia/gonorrhoea) – refer to GUM
  - Review for underlying cause: STI screen results, urine culture, check for prostatic obstruction (PSA, flow rate – usually after infection resolves)
  - Consider urology follow-up if recurrent, bilateral, or underlying structural abnormality
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann's 1 L over 8-12 hours if oral intake poor; if septic: consider bolus and reassess
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (or 20mg if <50kg/CrCl <30) – unless at high bleeding risk
  - **Diet:** as tolerated (ensure adequate fluid intake)
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly (if stable); 1-2 hourly if septic; monitor urine output
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **<35 years (presumed STI):** Doxycycline 100mg PO twice daily for 14 days **+** Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose (before doxycycline)
  - **≥35 years or positive urine culture:** Co-amoxiclav 500/125mg PO three times daily for 14 days
  - **If suspected pseudomonas (e.g. recent catheter, hospital-acquired):** Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO twice daily for 14 days (or [check local policy] for IV alternatives)
- **Alternatives:**
  - Levofloxacin 500mg PO once daily for 14 days (if penicillin allergy, but note fluoroquinolone restrictions)
  - Of course, adjust based on culture results
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Doxycycline: avoid antacids, iron, dairy within 2 hours; photosensitivity
  - Ceftriaxone: caution if penicillin anaphylaxis (see local policy); do not give with calcium-containing IV fluids in neonates (not relevant here)
  - Ciprofloxacin: risk of tendon rupture (especially >60 years, on steroids); avoid in myasthenia gravis; dose reduce in renal impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Doxycycline: no renal adjustment; caution in severe hepatic impairment
  - Co-amoxiclav: dose interval extended if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g. 500/125mg PO 12-hourly; for severe infection [check local policy])
  - Ciprofloxacin: CrCl 30-50: 500mg PO 12-hourly; CrCl <30: 500mg PO 24-hourly
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting ceftriaxone IM when treating a young man with suspected STI** – doxycycline alone does not cover *N. gonorrhoeae*; missing it risks treatment failure, spread, and serious complications (disseminated gonococcal infection). Always give ceftriaxone first if any suspicion.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Epididymal/testicular abscess – requires drainage (urology)
  - Pyocele – infected hydrocele, may need drain
  - Testicular infarction (due to severe inflammation, not torsion) – rare
  - Sepsis / septic shock
  - Acute urinary retention (especially if prostatitis)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic epididymo-orchitis – persistent pain >3 months
  - Epididymal obstruction → reduced fertility / azoospermia (if bilateral)
  - Chronic testicular pain (orchalgia)
  - Recurrent infections (if underlying obstruction/STI not treated)
sections.Prognosis:
- Excellent if treated early – most improve within 48-72 hours; full resolution in 2-4 weeks.
- Risk of infertility increases with bilateral involvement, recurrent episodes, or delayed treatment.
- Mortality is negligible unless septic shock or Fournier's (high mortality 20-50%).
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do scrotal Doppler ultrasound before leaving ED** – it's the standard of care to exclude torsion.
- **Ask about sexual history openly** – many young men will not volunteer it; STI is the most common cause in under 35s.
- **Prescribe scrotal support and NSAIDs** – reduces pain and speeds recovery.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not rely on Prehn's sign alone** – it has poor specificity; always get Doppler if any doubt.
- **Do not prescribe ciprofloxacin as first-line in young men** without checking for gonorrhoea/chlamydia cover – and beware tendon damage risk.
- **Do not forget to treat partners** – otherwise reinfection is likely; refer to GUM clinic.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - Worsening pain, fever, or new systemic symptoms (shivering, confusion)
  - Inability to pass urine (>8 hours with discomfort)
  - Skin changes on scrotum (redness spreading, black spots, crepitus)
  - Failure to improve after 48 hours of treatment
- **Follow-up:** 
  - GP in 1-2 weeks for symptom review and check urine culture results
  - GUM clinic if STI identified
  - Urology clinic if recurrent, bilateral, or structural abnormality suspected
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / urology immediately if:** 
  - Suspected testicular torsion (any age) – do not wait
  - Suspected Fournier’s gangrene
  - Scrotal abscess requiring drainage
  - Acute urinary retention (may need catheter)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 7 (or ≥ 5 with single parameter high)
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L despite fluids
  - Systolic BP < 90 mmHg despite 1 L fluid bolus
  - GCS < 13 or deteriorating consciousness
- **Specific thresholds for sepsis escalation:**
  - qSOFA ≥ 2 (RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered mentation)
  - Lactate > 2 mmol/L – consider organ support

rawMarkdown:
## Epididymo-Orchitis
**One-liner:** Acute infection/inflammation of epididymis +/- testis — young sexually active or older men with UTI risk; missed torsion = testicular loss.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Testicular torsion** – sudden onset (hours), severe pain, no fever, absent cremasteric reflex, high-riding testicle, negative Prehn's sign (pain unchanged with elevation). **Doppler ultrasound shows absent flow** → emergency exploration within 6 hours.
- **Fournier’s gangrene** – necrotising fasciitis of perineum; crepitus on palpation, systemic toxicity (tachycardia, hypotension, high lactate), dusky skin. **Immediate surgical debridement + IV antibiotics** – do not wait for imaging if clinical suspicion.
- **Sepsis** – fever, rigors, hypotension, raised lactate. **Start IV antibiotics and fluids within 1 hour** after blood cultures.

### Presentation
- **Symptoms:**
  - Gradual (over hours–days) unilateral testicular pain, swelling, erythema
  - Dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency
  - Urethral discharge (if STI-related)
  - Fever, malaise
- **Signs** (examine standing):
  - Swollen, tender epididymis (palpable separate from testis early; later both are involved)
  - Reactive hydrocele (transilluminates)
  - Prehn's sign: pain relieved by elevating scrotum (supports epididymitis, not torsion) – *not reliable alone, use Doppler*
  - Urethral discharge (milking urethra)
  - Suprapubic tenderness (if concurrent prostatitis)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Testicular torsion** ↔ sudden onset, no urinary symptoms, absent Doppler flow, high-riding testis
- **Testicular tumour** ↔ painless, hard, irregular mass, no fever/urinary symptoms, Doppler shows vascularity
- **Incarcerated/strangulated inguinal hernia** ↔ above the scrotum, not reducible, bowel sounds, obstruction symptoms
- **Acute hydrocele** ↔ fluctuant, transilluminates, non-tender
- **Mumps orchitis** ↔ post-pubertal, parotitis, bilateral (uncommon)
- **Trauma** ↔ history, bruising, Doppler normal
- **Splitter:** **Prehn's sign + Doppler ultrasound** – pain relief with scrotal elevation + normal/ increased Doppler flow = epididymitis; no relief + absent flow = torsion.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urinalysis (dipstick) – nitrites, leukocytes, blood
  - Urine pregnancy test if relevant (not primary) – actually skip; not needed here
  - Check testicular lie and cremasteric reflex
  - NEWS2 chart, temperature, blood pressure
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (white cell count, neutrophils)
  - CRP
  - Urea & Creatinine (if suspected sepsis or obstruction)
  - Blood cultures if febrile/septic
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line: Scrotal ultrasound with colour Doppler** – assess blood flow, size, abscess, pyocele
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis if suspect abscess or complicated UTI (older men with obstruction)
- **Special tests:**
  - **STI screen** (≤35 years or sexually active): first-void urine for *Chlamydia trachomatis* + *Neisseria gonorrhoeae* NAAT; urethral swab if discharge; also HIV/ syphilis serology
  - **Mid-stream urine culture** (MSU) – before antibiotics
- **Never-miss test:** **Scrotal Doppler ultrasound** – omission risks missing torsion (medicolegal gold standard). If unavailable in hours, discuss with urology/radiology – do not wait >6 hours if torsion suspected clinically.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Analgesia: paracetamol 1g IV/PO + ibuprofen 400mg PO (if no contraindication)
  - Scrotal support (elevated using towel/underwear)
  - **If torsion suspected:** inform urology immediately – do not wait for ultrasound if clinical suspicion high; transfer to theatre for exploration
  - **If signs of sepsis:** IV fluids (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline 500mL stat, repeat as per BP), IV antibiotics after cultures; escalate to sepsis 6
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Antibiotics according to presumed cause (see Drugs below)
  - If severe: continue IV until afebrile 48h, then switch to PO
  - Drainage of abscess (urology) if ultrasound shows collection
  - Monitor for urinary retention (common if prostatitis) – may need catheter
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Complete antibiotic course (usually 14 days)
  - Treat partner if STI diagnosed (chlamydia/gonorrhoea) – refer to GUM
  - Review for underlying cause: STI screen results, urine culture, check for prostatic obstruction (PSA, flow rate – usually after infection resolves)
  - Consider urology follow-up if recurrent, bilateral, or underlying structural abnormality
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or Hartmann's 1 L over 8-12 hours if oral intake poor; if septic: consider bolus and reassess
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (or 20mg if <50kg/CrCl <30) – unless at high bleeding risk
  - **Diet:** as tolerated (ensure adequate fluid intake)
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly (if stable); 1-2 hourly if septic; monitor urine output

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **<35 years (presumed STI):** Doxycycline 100mg PO twice daily for 14 days **+** Ceftriaxone 500mg IM single dose (before doxycycline)
  - **≥35 years or positive urine culture:** Co-amoxiclav 500/125mg PO three times daily for 14 days
  - **If suspected pseudomonas (e.g. recent catheter, hospital-acquired):** Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO twice daily for 14 days (or [check local policy] for IV alternatives)
- **Alternatives:**
  - Levofloxacin 500mg PO once daily for 14 days (if penicillin allergy, but note fluoroquinolone restrictions)
  - Of course, adjust based on culture results
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Doxycycline: avoid antacids, iron, dairy within 2 hours; photosensitivity
  - Ceftriaxone: caution if penicillin anaphylaxis (see local policy); do not give with calcium-containing IV fluids in neonates (not relevant here)
  - Ciprofloxacin: risk of tendon rupture (especially >60 years, on steroids); avoid in myasthenia gravis; dose reduce in renal impairment
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Doxycycline: no renal adjustment; caution in severe hepatic impairment
  - Co-amoxiclav: dose interval extended if CrCl <30 mL/min (e.g. 500/125mg PO 12-hourly; for severe infection [check local policy])
  - Ciprofloxacin: CrCl 30-50: 500mg PO 12-hourly; CrCl <30: 500mg PO 24-hourly
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting ceftriaxone IM when treating a young man with suspected STI** – doxycycline alone does not cover *N. gonorrhoeae*; missing it risks treatment failure, spread, and serious complications (disseminated gonococcal infection). Always give ceftriaxone first if any suspicion.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Epididymal/testicular abscess – requires drainage (urology)
  - Pyocele – infected hydrocele, may need drain
  - Testicular infarction (due to severe inflammation, not torsion) – rare
  - Sepsis / septic shock
  - Acute urinary retention (especially if prostatitis)
- **Chronic:**
  - Chronic epididymo-orchitis – persistent pain >3 months
  - Epididymal obstruction → reduced fertility / azoospermia (if bilateral)
  - Chronic testicular pain (orchalgia)
  - Recurrent infections (if underlying obstruction/STI not treated)

### Prognosis
- Excellent if treated early – most improve within 48-72 hours; full resolution in 2-4 weeks.
- Risk of infertility increases with bilateral involvement, recurrent episodes, or delayed treatment.
- Mortality is negligible unless septic shock or Fournier's (high mortality 20-50%).

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do scrotal Doppler ultrasound before leaving ED** – it's the standard of care to exclude torsion.
- **Ask about sexual history openly** – many young men will not volunteer it; STI is the most common cause in under 35s.
- **Prescribe scrotal support and NSAIDs** – reduces pain and speeds recovery.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not rely on Prehn's sign alone** – it has poor specificity; always get Doppler if any doubt.
- **Do not prescribe ciprofloxacin as first-line in young men** without checking for gonorrhoea/chlamydia cover – and beware tendon damage risk.
- **Do not forget to treat partners** – otherwise reinfection is likely; refer to GUM clinic.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - Worsening pain, fever, or new systemic symptoms (shivering, confusion)
  - Inability to pass urine (>8 hours with discomfort)
  - Skin changes on scrotum (redness spreading, black spots, crepitus)
  - Failure to improve after 48 hours of treatment
- **Follow-up:** 
  - GP in 1-2 weeks for symptom review and check urine culture results
  - GUM clinic if STI identified
  - Urology clinic if recurrent, bilateral, or structural abnormality suspected

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / urology immediately if:** 
  - Suspected testicular torsion (any age) – do not wait
  - Suspected Fournier’s gangrene
  - Scrotal abscess requiring drainage
  - Acute urinary retention (may need catheter)
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥ 7 (or ≥ 5 with single parameter high)
  - Lactate > 4 mmol/L despite fluids
  - Systolic BP < 90 mmHg despite 1 L fluid bolus
  - GCS < 13 or deteriorating consciousness
- **Specific thresholds for sepsis escalation:**
  - qSOFA ≥ 2 (RR ≥22, SBP ≤100, altered mentation)
  - Lactate > 2 mmol/L – consider organ support
